coredata.dc:title: A highly efficient molecularly imprinted fluorescence sensor for selective and sensitive detection of tetracycline antibiotic residues in pork
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Chao
Ma, Yangmin
Fan, Cheng
Kang, Jie
Ma, Siyue
coredata.dc:description: Tetracyclines (TCs) residues in animal-based foods may cause consumer poisoning or allergic reactions, as well as resistance of pathogenic microorganisms. Therefore, a method to accurately monitor TCs is urgently needed. Here, a sensitive and greenificated molecularly imprinted fluorescent sensor (CDs@MIP) for detecting TCs residues using non-covalent imprinting binding method has been synthesized in a simple and safe manner. The sensor was demonstrated to contain specific adsorption sites and high adsorption capacity by static adsorption test results and Freundlich and Langmuir model analyses, and the maximum adsorption capacity was 45.8 mg/g. Moreover, the sensor exhibited intense blue-green fluorescence under UV light, and the fluorescence was gradually quenched as TCs was adsorbed to achieve the detection. The fluorescent spectrum experiment demonstrated that the prepared sensor has good recognition and detection performance for TCs with a linear range of 0.02–50 mg/L and a detection limit of 0.18 μg/L. Furthermore, This sensor is recyclable and can be used to determine TCs content in pork with a recovery rate of 98 %–108 %. Therefore, the constructed sensor provides a new convenient strategy for detecting TCs residues.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Molecular imprinted
Fluorescence sensor
Adsorption
Tetracycline antibiotic
Carbon dots
Quenching

Detection of TCs in the real samples
Saturated adsorption capacity and isothermal adsorption model
Fluorescence detection of CDs@MIP toward TCs
Possible mechanism of the molecularly imprinted fluorescence sensor
Detection of tetracycline in the real samples
Comparison with MIP-based sensors for detecting TCs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV absorption and fluorescence characteristics of CDs; (b) CIE coordinate of CDs.
SEM image and particle size distribution of CDs@MIP (a) and CDs@NIP (b).
FT-IR spectrum of CDs@MIP and CDs@NIP.
(a) Static adsorption results; (b) Scatchard analysis results; (c) Freundlich adsorption model fitting results; (d) Langmuir adsorption model fitting results.

(a) Selectivity of CDs@MIP and CDs@NIP to some antibiotics; (b) Anti-interference; (c) Stability of CDs@MIP between batches (d) Reusability (e) The effect of pH on CDs@MIP performance (f) The effect of response time on CDs@MIP performance (TET: Tetracycline; CTE: Chlortetracycline; OXY: Oxytetracycline; AM: Amoxicillin; PC: Penicillin; CPC: Cephalosporin; GM: Gentamicin; AK: Amikacin; RXM: Roxithromycin; AZM: Azithromycin; NFX: Norfloxacin; CIX: Ciprofloxacin; SDS: Sulfadiazine).
The fluorescence spectra and corresponding fitting curves of CDs@MIP and CDs@NIP exposed to 0–50 mg/L TET and TCs (TET:CTE:OXY=1:1:1).
Specific adsorption and possible fluorescence sensing mechanisms of CDs@MIP.
The effect of TCs on the fluorescence lifetime of CDs@MIP.
Adsorption model parameters of CDs@MIP and CDs@NIP.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: n: Freundlich adsorption strength constant; Kf: adsorption capacity constant; Kl: adsorption equilibrium constant; Qm: maximum adsorption capacity.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The sensing of TCs in real samples through fluorescence analysis.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: TET: Tetracycline; CTE: Chlortetracycline; OXY: Oxytetracycline; HPLC: High performance liquid chromatography; RSD: Relative standard deviations.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of analytical performance of the CDs@MIP sensor with MIP-based sensors previously reported for TCs detection.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Y represents that the preparation of the sensor meets the requirements for GREENIFICATION; N represents that the preparation process of the sensor does not meet the requirements for GREENIFICATION.

head.ce:title: A highly efficient molecularly imprinted fluorescence sensor for selective and sensitive detection of tetracycline antibiotic residues in pork

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Chemical Additives for China National Light Industry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shaanxi University of Science and Technology, Xi'an, Shaanxi 710021, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Chemical Additives for China National Light Industry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shaanxi University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shaanxi Institute of Product Quality Supervision and Inspection, Xi'an, Shaanxi 710048, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shaanxi Institute of Product Quality Supervision and Inspection

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shaanxi Institute of Product Quality Supervision and Inspection, Xi'an, Shaanxi, 710048, China.

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: Shaanxi University of Science and Technology, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shaanxi University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tetracyclines (TCs) residues in animal-based foods may cause consumer poisoning or allergic reactions, as well as resistance of pathogenic microorganisms. Therefore, a method to accurately monitor TCs is urgently needed. Here, a sensitive and greenificated molecularly imprinted fluorescent sensor (CDs@MIP) for detecting TCs residues using non-covalent imprinting binding method has been synthesized in a simple and safe manner. The sensor was demonstrated to contain specific adsorption sites and high adsorption capacity by static adsorption test results and Freundlich and Langmuir model analyses, and the maximum adsorption capacity was 45.8 mg/g. Moreover, the sensor exhibited intense blue-green fluorescence under UV light, and the fluorescence was gradually quenched as TCs was adsorbed to achieve the detection. The fluorescent spectrum experiment demonstrated that the prepared sensor has good recognition and detection performance for TCs with a linear range of 0.02–50 mg/L and a detection limit of 0.18 μg/L. Furthermore, This sensor is recyclable and can be used to determine TCs content in pork with a recovery rate of 98 %–108 %. Therefore, the constructed sensor provides a new convenient strategy for detecting TCs residues.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Food safety problems, especially those caused by antimicrobial residues in animal-origin foods, are of great concern (Luo et al., 2022). Tetracyclines (TCs) are a class of lower-cost and broad-spectrum antibiotics, including tetracycline (TET), chlortetracycline (CTE), oxytetracycline (OXY) and doxycycline (DOX), that are extensively used to treat intestinal infections in animals or as feed additives to promote the growth of livestock (Valladales-Restrepo et al., 2022). Over 20 TCs have been widely approved for use in food-producing animals at subtherapeutic doses and function as growth promoters; and excessive antibiotics may remain in animal bodies and further appear in animal products available for human consumption, such as animal tissues, eggs, and milk (Pang et al., 2021). These residues may cause toxic or allergic reactions in some hypersensitive consumers and cause pathogenic organisms to gain resistance to these drugs (He et al., 2021). Considering this knowledge, a method to accurately monitor TCs is urgently needed by public health agencies in foodstuffs and environmental samples.

The traditional instrument detection methods used for TCs analysis have high detection costs and low efficiency due to the need for expensive equipment, complex pre-processing, and professional technical personnel (Fan et al., 2022). Enzyme linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) mainly utilizes specific antibodies/enzymes from organisms to specifically recognize target substances, and the cultivation, extraction, and purification steps of antibodies/enzymes from animal bodies undoubtedly increase economic costs. In addition, reagent kits based on ELISA technology have strict requirements for storage and use conditions, and often have a high false positive rate in practical applications (Wang et al., 2019).

Carbon dots (CDs) is a type of zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials, widely used in fields such as probes and sensing due to their good solubility, low toxicity, easy preparation and modification, high quantum yield, excellent optical stability, and biocompatibility (Wei et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2021). Some researchers have mixed CDs or CDs with lanthanide metal ions/gold nanoparticles/metal organic frameworks to construct single emission or ratio fluorescence sensor systems, achieving sensitive detection by directly mixing with TCs to induce fluorescence quenching (Mathew et al., 2023; Tantawy et al., 2020; Xie et al., 2022; Fan et al., 2022; Hu et al., 2020; Xing et al., 2020). Although the introduction of reference fluorescence overcomes the interference of the testing environment to some extent, the specificity of this direct mixed detection method needs to be improved.

Organic-based molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) developed based on the principle of antigen antibody has shown great potential as a recognition unit in sensing platforms, and it compensates for the shortcomings of natural antibodies such as poor physical and chemical stability, complex preparation, high cost, and difficult screening (Arabi et al., 2022), and has advantages such as high temperature resistance, high pressure resistance, acid and alkali resistance, and organic solvent resistance (Arabi and Chen, 2022). To date, various sensors based on MIT for antibiotic analysis have been widely studied, among which fluorescent sensors have attracted attention due to their high sensitivity (capable of single molecule detection), low cost, high efficiency, small sample size required, ability to provide sub nanometer spatial resolution, sub-micron visualization and sub millisecond time resolution, as well as tunable luminescent photophysical properties (Li et al., 2023). The gel-sol method was used to coat CDs with molecularly imprinted polymers (MIP) to prepare a fluorescence sensor for the detection of TCs (Wang et al., 2022), and this method uses toxic, highly volatile and irritating ammonia as an initiator, which is extremely harmful to human skin, eyes, and respiratory tract. During the imprinting process, covalent imprinting strategies often face difficulties in eluting template molecules, and the use of acidification reagents can easily damage the imprinting cavity and affect specific recognition; non-covalent imprinting strategies overcome these shortcomings, and most of the MIP prepared have reusable performance (Arabi et al., 2021). Very recently, greenificated MIP has gradually developed, and following the principles of economy, green, and sustainability to minimize the adverse effects of MIT on health and the environment has become a trend (Ostovan et al., 2022).

In this work, we synthesized a sensitive and greenificated MIP fluorescent sensor using a non-covalent imprinting strategy. The sensor was synthesized in simple step using inexpensive and relatively safe raw materials, which meets the requirements for GREENIFICATION. The ethanol elution process did not cause damage to the imprint cavity, ensuring stability during repeated use. Additionally, the low detection limit and wide linear range of the sensor make it highly sensitive and suitable for detecting TCs residues in various matrices. The mechanisms of fluorescence quenching and selectivity are discussed. This sensor has also been applied to the detection of TCs in real samples.

Methyl acrylic acid (MAA), thiourea and citric acid were purchased from Tianjin Tianli Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). 2,2′-Azobis(2-methylpropionitrile) (AIBN) was purchased from Anneji Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). Ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA) was obtained from Aladdin Industrial Corporation. Other chemicals were obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Deionized water was used in the whole experiment.

Ultraviolet−visible (UV−vis) spectra were collected by a Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrophotometer (Japan). The fluorescence measurements were recorded on an F97 XP fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shanghai, China). Hitachi SU8230 scanning electron microscope (SEM) is used to analyze morphological features (Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded with a Bruker TENSOR37 FT-IR spectrometer (Germany). The Shimadzu LC-20A high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) system consisted of LC-10ATVP pumps and a fluorescence detector (Japan). All separations were achieved on a C18 reversed-phase column (4.6×250 mm, 5 mm) at a mobile flow rate of 1.0 mL/min. The mobile phase was methanol/water (30:70, v/v).

Citric acid (1.0 g) and thiourea (1.5 g) were dissolved in 10 mL of water to form a transparent solution. Then, the solution was transferred into a poly(tetrafluoroethylene)-lined stainless-steel autoclave for the solvothermal reaction. After a heating treatment was performed at 180 °C for 12 h and the solution was cooled to room temperature, the obtained mixture was filtered through a filtration membrane (0.22 µm) to remove large particle residues. Afterward, the supernatant was collected by centrifugation (8944 g, 10 min, 4 °C) and dried under vacuum (-20 °C). Finally, nearly 1.0 g black powder (CDs) was obtained (Dong et al., 2023).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of molecularly imprinted fluorescence sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Molecular imprinted fluorescent sensors were prepared according to previously reported methods (Vahidifar and Es’haghi, 2022). TET (1.0 mmol) as a template molecule was mixed with 4.0 mmol MAA and dissolved in a round bottom flask containing 20 mL acetonitrile solution. Then, the solution was fully dissolved and ultrasonically treated for 5 min. Next, it was prepolymerized at 4 ℃ for 4 h, and 20 mg CDs, 20 mmol EDGMA and 15 mmol AIBN were added. Then, the solution was filled with nitrogen to remove oxygen for 10 min in an ice bath and polymerized at 60 ℃ for 24 h under magnetic stirring. Finally, the template molecules were washed with 100 mL ethanol until the template molecules could not be detected. As a comparison, the non-molecularly imprinted polymer sensors (CDs@NIP) were prepared and treated under the same conditions as CDs@MIP, except for the absence of template molecules.

The sample was fixed on the loading platform using conductive adhesive, and the morphology of the sample was observed by SEM after gold spraying. The FT-IR was collected on a KBr disk using an infrared spectrometer with a wavenumber range of 4000–500 cm−1.

Twenty milligrams of polymer were added into a 10 mL centrifuge tube, and 5 mL of TET standard solution with initial concentrations of 5, 10, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120, 140, 160, 180, 200, 250 and 300 mg/L was added. The mixture was shaken slowly for 2 h at 25 ℃ in the dark and then centrifuged at 4500 g for 5 min, and the content of TET in the supernatant was determined by HPLC. The adsorption capacity Q
e was calculated according to the following formula (1):
(1)
Q
e
=
C
0
−
C
e
V
M
where C 0 is the initial concentration of antibiotic solution; Ce is the equilibrium concentration of antibiotic solution; V is the volume of antibiotic solution; M is the mass of MIP/NIP; and Q
e is the adsorption capacity of TET after adsorption equilibrium.

Scatchard analysis was performed on the isothermal adsorption experimental data. The equation is shown in formula (2). Q e /C e was taken as the ordinate, and Q e was taken as the abscissa for fitting. Q m and Kd were calculated according to the fitting results.
(2)
Q
e
C
e
=
Q
m
−
Q
e
K
d
where Kd is the equilibrium dissociation constant of the binding site, mg/L, and Q m is the maximum adsorption capacity, mg/g.

Freundlich and Langmuir isothermal adsorption models were used to evaluate the static adsorption process. The fitting equation of the Freundlich adsorption model is shown in formula (3), with lnQ e as the ordinate and lnC e as the abscissa for fitting, and n and Kf were calculated according to the fitting equation.
(3)
ln
Q
e
=
ln
C
e
n
+ ln
K
f
where n is the Freundlich adsorption strength constant and Kf is the adsorption capacity constant, L/g.

The fitting equation of the Langmuir adsorption model is shown in formula (4), taking C e /Q
e as the ordinate and C e as the abscissa for fitting, and Q m and Kl were calculated according to the fitting equation.
(4)
C
e
Q
e
=
1
Q
m
K
l
+
C
e
Q
m
where Kl is the adsorption equilibrium constant.

Antibiotic standard solution was added to a 4 mg/mL CDs@MIP aqueous solution according to the research objectives. The solutions remained static for 90 min under dark conditions. After ultrasonic treatment for 1 min, the fluorescence spectra of the solution were promptly recorded at room temperature with excitation at 396 nm, and the slit widths of excitation and emission were both 5 nm. The fluorescence intensity without antibiotics is represented as F0, and that in the presence of antibiotics is denoted as F. The selectivity and anti-interference of CDs@MIP was investigated using other antibiotics under the same testing conditions. The reaction time was optimized through examining the change in the F/F0 signal with time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TCs in the real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the practicality of CDs@MIP in real food samples, 50 pork muscle tissue was purchased from nearby supermarkets and made into minced meat using a crusher. Approximate 5 g of sample was accurately weighed in a centrifuge tube. Then, 10 mL of 2 % lead acetate solution was added, shaken for 10 min, and centrifuge 1400 g at 4 ℃ for 10 min, and TCs in the supernatant were analyzed by a fluorescence sensor. TET, OXY and CTE were used as targets to determine the recovery rate. All experiments were repeated 3 times.

A simple and green hydrothermal method was used to prepare CDs. The method was then optically characterized using a variety of techniques. The quantum yield of the CDs was calculated and found to be 16.5 % by using quinine sulfate as a reference. The CDs display blue-green fluorescence under an ultraviolet lamp. The best excitation wavelength and best emission wavelength of CDs are 396 nm and 496 nm ( Fig. 1a), respectively, as analyzed by fluorescence spectrometry. As shown in Fig. 1b, the coordinates of the fluorescent color of CDs in the CIE (Commission Internationale de L′Eclairage) standard colorimetric system are (0.23, 0.46).

The morphologies of CDs@MIP and CDs@NIP were investigated by SEM, and the particle size was measured by image J software, and the particle size distribution was plotted. As shown in Fig. 2, the synthesized CDs@MIP and CDs@NIP are irregular particles with a particle size of 2.8–40 μm. CDs@MIP and CDs@NIP had similar particle size distribution, and the average particle size was 16.2 μm and 15.8 μm, respectively. CDs@MIP was analogs to a sponge, and its structure was fluffy, hollow, and full of small holes inside (illustration). As a result, many adsorption sites are available for the binding of TCs. CDs@NIP has a rough surface, irregular structure, and clusters. The results indicate that CDs@MIP was successfully constructed.

The FT-IR spectrometer was utilized to analyze the surface functional groups of CDs@MIP and CDs@NIP, as exhibited in Fig. 3. The signal bands at 3533 cm−1, 3298 cm−1, 1267 cm−1 and 1145 cm−1 were ascribed to the -NH2, -NH- and -OH group bending and stretching vibrations, respectively (Shirani et al., 2021). The peaks at 2997 cm−1, 2954 cm−1 and 1485 cm−1 were symmetric and asymmetric tensile and flexural vibration peaks of -CH2-, which indicated that EGDMA was involved in the CDs@MIP synthetic reaction (Mardani et al., 2020; Li et al., 2022). The strong absorption peak at 1731 cm−1 may be caused by the CO bond in the MAA molecule (Yuan et al., 2021). The absorption peak at 2590 cm−1 may be associated with the breaking of the CS bond on CDs to generate -SH. The difference is that the CDs@MIP adsorbed with TET exhibited a new absorption peak at 2364 cm−1, which is related to the stretching vibration of -N-CO on the tetracycline molecule. The above analysis demonstrated that CDs@MIP and CDs@NIP were successfully prepared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Saturated adsorption capacity and isothermal adsorption model
ce:section.ce:para: The equilibrium adsorption capacities of CDs@MIP and CDs@NIP for TET solutions of different concentrations are shown in Fig. 4a. The equilibrium adsorption capacity of TET increased gradually when the initial concentration was increased from 5.0 mg/L to 300 mg/L. When the TET initial concentration was 250 mg/L, the equilibrium adsorption capacity reached the saturation adsorption capacity, which was 45.8 mg/g and 9.5 mg/g.

The adsorption type, adsorption binding site type and distribution of MIP can be determined by the Scatchcard equation (Bi et al., 2022). The larger the saturated adsorption capacity is, the stronger the adsorption capacity of MIP per unit mass. In contrast, the smaller the dissociation constant value is, the stronger the affinity of MIP (Kouki et al., 2022). As shown in Fig. 4b, based on the two fitting curves of CDs@MIP, there are two kinds of binding sites with high affinity and low affinity on the surface of CDs@MIP. In contrast to CDs@MIP, the CDs@NIP surface only contains a kind of binding site. The Kd values of CDs@MIP and CDs@NIP were calculated as 0.46 mg/L and 111.0 mg/L, respectively. It was reported that the smaller the Kd is, the stronger the adsorption (Xiong et al., 2022). The Q m values of CDs@MIP and CDs@NIP were 48.3 mg/g and 15.0 mg/g respectively. which was close to the experimental results.

According to the Freundlich and Langmuir isothermal adsorption models, the relationship between the equilibrium adsorption capacity and the concentration of TET in the solution after adsorption equilibrium is shown in Fig. 4c and d. The experimental result obtained for CDs@MIP could be well described by the Langmuir and Freundlich models (Langmuir, r 2=0.9982; Freundlich, r 2=0.9978). This result indicated that both physical adsorption and chemical adsorption occur on the CDs@MIP surface. Based on the Langmuir adsorption formula, the Q
max of CDs@MIP and CDs@NIP was calculated to be 47.6 mg/g and 12.9 mg/g ( Table 1), respectively, which is close to the experimental results. The range of 1/n from 0.1 to 0.5 indicated that MIP exhibits a good adsorption capacity (Sun et al., 2020). The 1/n of CDs@MIP was calculated to be 0.26 according to the Freundlich adsorption formula, while the 1/n of CDs@NIP was calculated to be 0.58, indicating that CDs@MIP exhibits a better adsorption capacity than that of CDs@NIP (Table 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of CDs@MIP toward TCs
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of fluorescence sensors is very important for the fluorescence detection. 10 μL of different antibiotic standard solutions with the same concentration were added to 5 mL of 4 mg/mL CDs@MIP and CDs@NIP aqueous solutions, respectively. After equilibrium, the selectivity of the sensor was evaluated by measuring the fluorescence intensity. As shown in Fig. 5a, the fluorescence intensity of the system of CDs@MIP with TET, OXY and OTC was significantly reduced compared with that of other antibiotics (p<0.05). Based on the TET, OXY and CTE have the same four-membered parent structure and similar functional groups that can be specifically identified, resulting in better class specificity of the CDs@MIP. In contrast, none of antibiotics significantly changed the fluorescence intensity of CDs@NIP without recognition holes.

Based on the good selectivity of CDs@MIP, the anti-interference capability was investigated as follows. As shown in Fig. 5b, the fluorescence intensity of the system containing TET was significantly reduced under the interference of different types of antibiotics. This result is due to the presence of specific tetracycline imprinted cavities on the molecularly imprinted sensor, which is conducive to its selective binding and specific adsorption.

To obtain fluorescent sensors with stable quality and excellent detection performance, the stability between batches was further studied. The fluorescence intensity of the CDs@MIP solution without TET and with 100 mg/L TET was measured to evaluate fluorescence stability, and the maximum adsorption capacity was evaluated using static adsorption tests (Fig. 5c). It can be seen from Fig. 5c that the fluorescence sensors across 5 batches exhibited stable fluorescence intensity and adsorption capacity. The relative standard deviations (RSD) of fluorescence intensity for CDs@MIP solutions without TET and containing 100 mg/L TET are 1.23 % and 1.45 %, respectively, and the RSD of maximum adsorption capacity is 1.63 %.

In addition, the circular practicability of CDs@MIP was studied. Twenty milligrams of CDs@MIP were put into a solution containing 100 mg/L TET and reacted for 2 h, and the adsorbed amount of CDs@MIP was recorded through HPLC. Afterward, an ethanol solution was used for elution until TET could not be detected. The fluorescence intensity of CDs@MIP was measured by a fluorescence spectrometer. The above steps were repeated. As shown in Fig. 5d, the results of six repeated experiments indicated that CDs@MIP exhibits good stability and reusability. However, a large amount of ethanol is required for template molecular elution in each cycle, which needs to be further optimized in the future.

The effects of pH and response time on CDs@MIP detection performance were further investigated, as shown in Fig. 5e and f. It can be seen from Fig. 5e that pH has a significant impact on the fluorescence intensity of CDs@MIP. The fluorescence intensity of CDs@MIP solutions containing and without TCs shows a similar trend with changes in pH. When the pH is between 6 and 7, the fluorescence intensity tends to stabilize, and when the pH is below 6 or above 7, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreases. The effect of response time on the fluorescence intensity of CDs@MIP was shown in Fig. 5f. The fluorescence intensity of CDs@MIP solution without TCs remains almost unchanged, whereas the fluorescence intensity of CDs@MIP solution containing 100 mg/L TCs gradually decreases until it stabilizes at 90 min. This response time has significant advantages compared to instrument detection, but it needs to be further optimized for rapid on-site detection in the future. Therefore, all subsequent experiments were conducted at pH 7 for 90 min for fluorescence recording.

To verify the potential of CDs@MIP for the quantitative detection of TCs, the fluorescent titration experiments were conducted. As shown in Fig. 6, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs@MIP at 496 nm decreased significantly with the increasing concentrations of TET and TCs (0–50 mg/L). CDs@NIP exhibited a slight decrease in fluorescence intensity as the concentration of TET and TCs increased. The titration results in the range of 0.02–50 mg/L TCs were further linearly fitted with F0/F as the ordinate and lnC as the abscissa. The determination coefficients (r 2) of CDs@MIP were all over than 0.9985. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated (3δ/k) to be 0.18 μg/L (k is the slope of the calibration line, δ is the standard deviation of blank measurement (n=20)). This predominant analysis performance may be related to the knowledge that oxygen-containing groups on the CD surface provide more active sites for the response of TCs (Fan et al., 2022). Furthermore, the sensing system has a simple operation procedure without strict conditions or perilous reagents, which can effectively overcome difficulties reported in the past.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of the molecularly imprinted fluorescence sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the excellent recognition performance of CDs@MIP needs to be explained. The template molecules first self-assemble with functional monomers to form polymers in the process of MIP preparation; this process is mainly based on the hydrogen bond force. Then the molecules react with the crosslinking agent to form CDs@MIP with a rigid structure under the action of the initiator. The template components are removed from the polymer molecules through the elution process. Next, the CDs@MIP surface forms binding holes that match the shape and size of the template molecules present. It can specifically recognize and adsorb on the MIP surface when template molecules exist. However, TET, OXY and CTE exhibit very high similarity, including the same quaternary ring structure and similar chemical groups (as shown in the Fig. 6); thus, they can enter the imprinted hole and be recognized by CDs@MIP ( Fig. 7).

The sensing of TCs using CDs@MIP was accomplished through the fluorescence quenching process. A good linear relationship between quenching intensity and tetracycline concentration was observed in a 0.02–50 mg/L range with a lower detection limit (0.18 μg/L). The possible fluorescence quenching mechanism between CDs@MIP and TCs was further discussed. As shown in Fig. 1a, TET has two obvious ultraviolet absorption peaks, and a good spectral overlap was observed between the ultraviolet absorption peak at 350 nm and the excitation spectrum of carbon quantum dots. This confirms the possibility of the following mechanisms: the inner filter effect (IFE) or/and Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET). IFE is an apparent quenching in which a second absorber filters the emission, resulting in an attenuation of the excited beam (Peng et al., 2022), whereas FRET is a phenomenon of energy transfer between the excited state of the carbon dot and the ground state of the quencher (Hao et al., 2022). The fluorescence lifetime of the carbon dot-quencher system will decrease or increase with FRET, whereas it remains the same or nearly unchanged with IFE (Yuan et al., 2017). The fluorescence internal filtration effect led to fluorescence quenching of the system by measuring the fluorescence lifetime changes of carbon quantum dots before and after the addition of TCs (Korah et al., 2022). The effect of tetracycline on the fluorescence lifetime of the sensor was further determined ( Fig. 8), and the results showed that the fluorescence lifetime before and after adsorption of TCs was 10.4 ns, demonstrated that fluorescence quenching is caused by the IFE mechanism. When the excitation light was irradiated, the continuously adsorbed and accumulated TCs on the CDs@MIP surface absorbed part of the excitation light, resulting in a decrease in the incident light absorbed by the CDs and a significant decrease in the fluorescence of the system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of tetracycline in the real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To ensure the practicability of the molecularly imprinted fluorescence sensor in real situations, the standard addition method was used to determine the recovery rate in pork samples using TET, OXY, and CTE as standard substances, and the results were compared with those determined by HPLC. All the experiments were carried out three times, and the summarized results are presented in Table 2. The detection results were close to the spiked values, with recovery rates ranging from 98.0 % to 108.0 % and relative standard deviations (RSD) lower than 4.95 %. Therefore, CDs@MIP is not affected by matrix interference in the detection of TCs, indicating that this sensor and analytical method possess high reliability and precision. Moreover, all the results offered by our method were in good agreement with those validated by HPLC. Thus, the sensor can be used to detect antibiotics in real samples. This sensor exhibits several advantages, as it is nontoxic, easy to operate, reusable, and environmentally friendly. As a result, it can be utilized in real samples without causing any impact on the ecosystem.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison with MIP-based sensors for detecting TCs
ce:section.ce:para: Compared with previously reported electrochemical and fluorescent sensors based on MIP, the prepared sensor has a lower detection limit and a wider linear range, making it more suitable for the detection of TCs in various matrices ( Table 3). In addition, the use of harmful reagents such as volatile toxins (Li et al., 2024; Liu et al., 2014), strong acids (Wang et al., 2011a) and bases (Chen et al., 2023) that may harm health and the environment, as well as the cumbersome and complex preparation process (Li et al., 2023; Xu et al., 2016), have been reported in literature during sensor preparation and template elution processes. The CDs@MIP preparation process is simple and uses inexpensive and almost harmless raw materials, which better meets the requirements of GREENIFICATION.

In summary, we synthesized a sensitive and greenificated MIP-based fluorescence sensor for the detection of TCs using a non-covalent imprinting strategy and a safe approach. The sensor was demonstrated to contain specific adsorption sites and high adsorption capacity by static adsorption test results and Freundlich and Langmuir model analyses. As a fluorescence sensor, CDs@MIP can detect TCs through fluorescence quenching with high selectivity and sensitivity, and the detection limit is as low as 0.18 μg/L. All these observations and analyses of sample analysis support that CDs@MIP is very economical due to its multiple recyclability and detection stability. With the development of technology, while considering economy and greenification, rapid, convenient, and intelligent sensors should be developed in the near future.

Chao Li: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Cheng Fan: Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis. Yangmin Ma: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Jie Kang: Writing – original draft, Methodology. Siyue Ma: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22178205); Postdoctoral Research Foundation of China (2022M711994); Service Local Special Plan of Education Department of Shaanxi Provincial Government (23JC015); Science and Technology Plan Project of Xi’an (21NYYF0057); Science and Technology Plan Project of Weiyang District (202114); Agricultural Science and Technology Innovation Project of Shaanxi Province (NYKJ-2022-XA-010); Shaanxi Provincial Administration for Market Regulation Science and Technology Plan Project (2023KY04).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Silanized carbon dot-based thermo-sensitive molecularly imprinted fluorescent sensor for bovine hemoglobin detection
